Retaliation against Neighboring Countries: If Iran loses control of the island and, consequently, the ability to export its oil, the regime could adopt a posture of total sabotage, attacking the oil infrastructure of other Gulf countries under the logic of "if I can't export, nobody can."

Mr. Zakaria,

I have followed your program for many years and have always respected the depth of your analysis and your ability to explain complex international issues. That is why I am writing with some concern after watching your recent commentary.

I want to be clear about where I am coming from. I am an American, but I am also someone who is deeply opposed to the ruling clerical regime in Iran. My perspective is shaped by the forty-seven years since the 1979 revolution, when the Islamic Republic came to power and fundamentally altered the relationship between Iran and the United States. Since the hostage crisis under President Jimmy Carter, successive administrations of both parties have struggled with the same reality: a regime that has defined itself through hostility toward the United States while projecting influence across the region through proxy conflicts and destabilizing actions. The financial and security costs of that reality have been enormous.

I am not a traditional Trump supporter. In fact, I did not approach this election from a place of loyalty to him. Like many independents, I ultimately voted for him largely out of frustration with the Democratic Party. What I hear repeatedly from Democratic leaders and commentators is not a clear strategic vision, but primarily criticism of Donald Trump himself. At some point that becomes politically counterproductive, and in many ways that constant focus on attacking him is precisely what has helped keep him politically viable.

When it comes to Iran, the issue is larger than American domestic politics. The clerical government and the Iranian people are not the same thing. More than ninety million Iranians live under that system, and many clearly do not support it. I speak regularly with people inside Iran as well as members of the Iranian diaspora, and what they describe often feels very different from the way the situation is presented in much of the American media. That disconnect is troubling.

For many years CNN had a reputation for presenting issues in a balanced and analytical way. Unfortunately, many viewers now feel that balance has been lost. The decline in viewership reflects that perception. I continue to watch your program on YouTube because I always believed you were one of the voices capable of bringing nuance and balance back into the discussion.

That is why your recent commentary surprised me. In moments of serious geopolitical tension, it is not always realistic or wise for a president to publicly lay out every detail of strategy to satisfy the media cycle. Leaders sometimes use ambiguity, messaging, and even misdirection as part of how they manage adversaries. Donald Trump’s style is unconventional and often provocative, but style and strategy are not the same thing.

Many Americans are less focused on rhetoric than on results. If a president is attempting to confront a geopolitical problem that has persisted for nearly half a century, it seems reasonable to allow some time before declaring failure. If the policy does not work, criticism will certainly be warranted. But if it does produce results, that should also be acknowledged.

I say this respectfully as someone who has followed your work for a long time. Viewers like me are looking for analysis that rises above partisan framing. I had hoped your voice would help restore some balance to the conversation. If that balance disappears, many longtime viewers will begin to disengage, and I would regret seeing that happen.

Respectfully.

Jazz improvisation isn’t chaos or reckless spontaneity. It’s the result of deep study, cultural heritage, and respect for a rich musical tradition shaped by generations of artists. Improvisation in jazz is disciplined freedom — musicians listening to each other, responding, creating together within a shared language.

Jazz was born from struggles for dignity and expression. It carries the history of liberty, social equality, and the democratic idea that every voice in the ensemble matters.
